description:
You are a network engineer who has been asked to confirm that the local network stack is alive and to demonstrate basic process control skills while doing so.  
Work inside the home directory of the default user (/home/user).  
Create a brand-new directory called “/home/user/netdiag”.

1. Launch a continuous (non-terminating) ICMP echo request (ping) toward the loopback interface (127.0.0.1) **as a background process**.  
   • Redirect both stdout and stderr of that ping process into the file  
     “/home/user/netdiag/ping.log”.  
   • Immediately after starting the command, record its PID in a one-line
     file called “/home/user/netdiag/ping.pid”.  
     Format of ping.pid must be **exactly**:  
       PID=<decimal_integer_value>

2. Allow the ping to run for **at least three seconds** and then stop it cleanly from the shell by sending it the default SIGINT (kill -2) or SIGTERM (kill -15).  
   • Do NOT use sudo.  
   • Make sure there is *no* residual “ping 127.0.0.1” process listed by the
     time you finish the task.

3. Capture a one-time snapshot of the process table immediately **before** you kill ping and store it in  
   “/home/user/netdiag/proc_before_kill.txt”.  
   The file must be the exact literal output of  
     ps -e -o pid,ppid,comm  
   (do not add or remove columns and do not re-format the output).

4. After the ping process has been stopped, create the final report
   “/home/user/netdiag/diag_report.log” in the following **mandatory
   3-line key/value format** (each element on its own line, no extra
   whitespace, no surrounding quotes):

   ping_pid:<decimal_integer_value>
   ping_killed:yes
   exit_status:0

   • `ping_pid` must match the same number that appeared in ping.pid.  
   • `exit_status` must be the numeric exit code emitted by your *kill*
     command (it should be 0 when the signal is delivered successfully).  

5. Leave all the above-mentioned files in place.  
   The automated grader will validate success by checking that:

   • “/home/user/netdiag” exists.  
   • “/home/user/netdiag/ping.log” is non-empty and contains the string “127.0.0.1”.  
   • “/home/user/netdiag/ping.pid” exists and follows the required PID=<number> format.  
   • “/home/user/netdiag/proc_before_kill.txt” exists and shows a line containing the ping PID and the word “ping”.  
   • “/home/user/netdiag/diag_report.log” exists and consists of exactly the three lines in the exact order and format shown above, with a numeric PID and the literal tokens “yes” and “0” as the final two values.  

You may use any standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities (ping, ps, kill, sleep, cat, etc.).  
No root privileges are required, and you should not modify anything outside “/home/user/netdiag”.
truth:
Expected filesystem objects and verification anchors that the grader will look for:

1. Directory:
   /home/user/netdiag         (must exist; any Unix-style 755 permissions are OK)

2. Files and required content:
   a) /home/user/netdiag/ping.log  
      • must be non-empty  
      • must contain the literal substring “127.0.0.1”

   b) /home/user/netdiag/ping.pid  
      • first (and only) line must match regex ^PID=[0-9]+$

   c) /home/user/netdiag/proc_before_kill.txt  
      • must contain a line where the first numerical field equals the number from ping.pid  
      • that same line must also contain the substring “ping”

   d) /home/user/netdiag/diag_report.log  
      • must contain exactly three newline-terminated lines matching these regex patterns **in order**  
          1) ^ping_pid:[0-9]+$  
          2) ^ping_killed:yes$  
          3) ^exit_status:0$  

3. Process state:
   • By the time grading starts, there must NOT be any live process whose command line includes “ping 127.0.0.1”.

No other files or directories are evaluated.  
The agent has permission to create the directory “/home/user/netdiag” and all
listed files.
